name: browser_type
docs:
## Purpose

Type text into a form input, textarea, or contenteditable element identified by a CSS selector. The field is **cleared first** by default — the previous content is completely replaced with the new text.

## Parameters

| Name | Type | Required | Description |
|------|------|----------|-------------|
| `selector` | string | **Yes** | CSS selector of the input element to type into. |
| `text` | string | **Yes** | The text to type into the element. |
| `clear` | boolean | No | When `true` (default), clears the field before typing — replacing existing content. When `false`, appends the text character-by-character via `pressSequentially` without clearing. Default: **true**. |

## When to Use

- **Filling out forms** — entering usernames, passwords, search queries, or any text input.
- **Testing input validation** — typing various values to test form validation behavior.
- **Search functionality** — entering search terms in search boxes.
- **Editing content** — modifying text in contenteditable regions or text editors.
- **Appending text** — use `clear: false` to add text to existing content without replacing it.

## When NOT to Use

- **Clicking buttons or links** — use `browser_click`.
- **When the input hasn't loaded yet** — use `browser_wait` first.
- **For programmatic value setting** — if you need to set a value without typing events (e.g., for hidden inputs), use `browser_eval` with JavaScript.

## Examples

```
# Type into a search box (replaces any existing text)
browser_type(selector="input[name='search']", text="WebSocket reconnection patterns")

# Fill a login form
browser_type(selector="#username", text="admin@example.com")
browser_type(selector="#password", text="secure-password-123")

# Type into a textarea
browser_type(selector="textarea.comment-input", text="This implementation looks correct.")

# Append text without clearing the field
browser_type(selector="input[name='search']", text=" additional terms", clear=false)

# Type into a contenteditable div
browser_type(selector="[contenteditable='true']", text="New paragraph content")

# Explicitly clear and replace (same as default)
browser_type(selector="#email", text="new@example.com", clear=true)
```

## Notes / Gotchas

- **Clears by default** — the existing field content is replaced entirely unless you pass `clear: false`.
- **Append mode** — with `clear: false`, text is typed character-by-character via `pressSequentially`, appending to whatever is already in the field. This fires individual key events for each character.
- **Fires input events** — typing triggers `input`, `change`, and `keydown`/`keyup` events, so JavaScript handlers attached to the input will fire.
- **Element must be focusable** — the target must be an `<input>`, `<textarea>`, or element with `contenteditable`. Typing into a `<div>` without contenteditable won't work.
- **Wait for dynamic inputs** — if the input appears after a page transition or async load, use `browser_wait(selector=...)` before typing.
- **Special characters** — most characters work as expected. For special keys (Enter, Tab, Escape), use `browser_eval` to dispatch keyboard events.

name: read
docs:
## Purpose

Read the contents of a file from disk into the conversation context. This is the primary tool for examining existing code, configuration, and documentation before making changes.

The tool handles both text files and images. For text files, contents are returned as plain text with line numbers available for reference. For images (jpg, png, gif, webp), the file is sent as a visual attachment that can be analyzed directly.

Output is automatically truncated to **2000 lines or 50KB** (whichever limit is hit first). For files exceeding these limits, use the `offset` and `limit` parameters to paginate through the content in chunks.

## Parameters

| Name | Type | Required | Description |
|------|------|----------|-------------|
| `path` | string | **Yes** | Path to the file to read. Can be relative (resolved from session working directory) or absolute. Supports forward slashes on all platforms. |
| `offset` | number | No | Line number to start reading from (**1-indexed**). Omit to start from the beginning. Use with `limit` for pagination. |
| `limit` | number | No | Maximum number of lines to return. Omit to read until truncation limit. Combine with `offset` to read specific ranges. |

## When to Use

- **Before editing a file** — always read the file (or the relevant section) first to get the exact text for the `oldText` parameter in `edit`.
- **Exploring unfamiliar codebases** — read entry points, config files, and key modules to understand structure.
- **Checking current state** — verify file contents after a write or edit to confirm the change took effect.
- **Reading large files in chunks** — use `offset`/`limit` to paginate through files that exceed the 2000-line truncation.
- **Examining images** — read screenshots, diagrams, or UI assets to understand visual context.

## When NOT to Use

- **Searching across many files** — use `bash` with `rg` (ripgrep) or `grep` instead. Reading files one by one to find a pattern is extremely slow.
- **Getting a directory listing** — use `bash` with `ls` or `find` instead.
- **Reading command output** — use `bash` directly; don't write to a file then read it.
- **Binary files** (other than supported images) — the output will be garbled. Use `bash` with `xxd` or `file` for binary inspection.

## Examples

```
# Read a full source file before making edits
read(path="src/server/server.ts")

# Read a specific function in a large file (lines 150-200)
read(path="src/app/remote-agent.ts", offset=150, limit=50)

# Continue reading a truncated file from where it left off
read(path="src/app/main.ts", offset=2001, limit=2000)

# Read a config file to check current settings
read(path="tsconfig.server.json")

# Read a screenshot to analyze UI state
read(path="tests/screenshots/error-state.png")
```

## Notes / Gotchas

- **Truncation is silent** — if the file is longer than 2000 lines, you only see the first 2000. Always check if the output seems cut off and continue with `offset` if needed.
- **Offset is 1-indexed** — line 1 is the first line of the file, not line 0.
- **Prefer `read` over `bash` with `cat`** — `read` handles encoding, pagination, and image detection automatically. Using `cat` in bash loses these features.
- **Parallel reads are fast** — if you need to read multiple files, launch all `read` calls in a single message rather than sequentially. This is much faster than delegating file reads.
- **Image support** — only jpg, png, gif, and webp are supported as visual attachments. Other formats (svg, pdf) are returned as text.
- **Path resolution** — relative paths resolve from the session's working directory. Use forward slashes even on Windows (bash-style paths work everywhere).

name: browser_eval
docs:
## Purpose

Execute arbitrary JavaScript in the context of the currently loaded browser page. The expression runs with full access to the page's DOM, window object, and any loaded scripts/frameworks. The return value is serialized and returned as a string.

This is the most flexible browser tool — anything you can do in a browser console, you can do with `browser_eval`.

## Parameters

| Name | Type | Required | Description |
|------|------|----------|-------------|
| `expression` | string | **Yes** | JavaScript expression or code to evaluate. The return value of the last expression is captured. For complex objects, use `JSON.stringify()`. |

## When to Use

- **Extracting data from the page** — reading text content, counting elements, checking attribute values.
- **Checking application state** — accessing framework state stores, localStorage, session data.
- **Complex interactions** — triggering events, scrolling, manipulating the DOM in ways that click/type can't handle.
- **Assertions and verification** — confirming that specific conditions are met on the page.
- **Debugging** — inspecting the page's JavaScript environment.

## When NOT to Use

- **Simple clicking** — use `browser_click` for clicking elements.
- **Simple typing** — use `browser_type` for entering text into inputs.
- **Taking screenshots** — use `browser_screenshot`.
- **When no page is loaded** — navigate first with `browser_navigate`.

## Examples

```
# Get the page title
browser_eval(expression="document.title")

# Count items in a list
browser_eval(expression="document.querySelectorAll('.todo-item').length")

# Extract text content from an element
browser_eval(expression="document.querySelector('.error-message')?.textContent?.trim()")

# Get application state as JSON
browser_eval(expression="JSON.stringify(window.__APP_STATE__, null, 2)")

# Scroll to the bottom of the page
browser_eval(expression="window.scrollTo(0, document.body.scrollHeight); 'scrolled'")

# Check localStorage values
browser_eval(expression="JSON.stringify(Object.fromEntries(Object.entries(localStorage)))")

# Trigger a custom event
browser_eval(expression="document.querySelector('#app').dispatchEvent(new CustomEvent('refresh')); 'dispatched'")
```

## Notes / Gotchas

- **Return value serialization** — primitive values (strings, numbers, booleans) are returned directly. Objects and arrays must be `JSON.stringify()`'d or they'll show as `[object Object]`.
- **Async expressions** — if your expression returns a Promise, the tool waits for it to resolve. You can use `await` in the expression: `await fetch('/api/data').then(r => r.json())`.
- **Error handling** — if the expression throws, the error message is returned. Wrap risky code in try/catch for cleaner error handling.
- **Page context only** — the expression runs in the page's JavaScript context, not Node.js. Browser APIs are available; Node.js APIs are not.
- **Side effects persist** — DOM changes, variable assignments, and event listeners created by `browser_eval` persist in the page until the next navigation.
- **Security** — the expression has full access to the page's JavaScript context. Be careful with sensitive data.

name: write
docs:
## Purpose Write content to a file on disk. If the file already exists, it is **completely overwritten**. If it doesn't exist, it is created along with any necessary parent directories. This is a full-file operation — the entire content you provide becomes the new file contents. There is no append mode or partial write. For modifying specific parts of an existing file, use `edit` instead. Files written with `.html` or `.svg` extensions are rendered inline in the Bobbit chat UI as live previews, making this tool ideal for creating visual mockups, reports, and data visualizations. ## Parameters | Name | Type | Required | Description | |------|------|----------|-------------| | `path` | string | **Yes** | Path to the file to write. Relative paths resolve from the session working directory. Parent directories are created automatically. | | `content` | string | **Yes** | The complete content to write to the file. This replaces any existing file content entirely. | ## When to Use - **Creating new files** — new source files, config files, test fixtures, scripts. - **Complete file rewrites** — when the changes are so extensive that surgical edits would be impractical (more than ~50% of the file is changing). - **Generating reports or visualizations** — write `.html` files for interactive previews in the chat. - **Creating test fixtures** — write JSON, HTML, or other data files needed by tests. - **Writing scripts** — create bash scripts, node scripts, or other executable files. ## When NOT to Use - **Small changes to existing files** — use `edit` instead. Overwriting a 500-line file to change 3 lines is risky (you might accidentally drop content) and wasteful. - **Appending to a file** — read the file first, then write the combined content. Or use `bash` with `echo >> file`. - **Binary files** — use `bash` with appropriate tools (e.g., `cp`, `curl -o`) for binary content. ## Examples ``` # Create a new TypeScript module write(path="src/utils/format-date.ts", content="export function formatDate(d: Date): string {\n return d.toISOString().split('T')[0];\n}\n") # Create a test fixture write(path="tests/fixtures/sample-config.json", content="{\n \"port\": 3000,\n \"host\": \"localhost\",\n \"debug\": true\n}") # Write an HTML report that renders inline in chat write(path="report.html", content="\n\n

Test Results

All 42 tests passed.

") # Create a shell script write(path="scripts/deploy.sh", content="#!/bin/bash\nset -euo pipefail\nnpm run build\nnpm run test\necho 'Deploy complete'") # Create parent directories automatically write(path="src/features/auth/handlers/login.ts", content="export async function handleLogin(req, res) {\n // ...\n}") ``` ## Notes / Gotchas - **Overwrites without warning** — there is no confirmation prompt. If the file exists, it is replaced entirely. Read the file first if you need to preserve parts of it. - **Parent directory creation** — all intermediate directories are created automatically. No need to `mkdir -p` first. - **Inline rendering** — `.html` and `.htm` files render in a sandboxed iframe in the chat. `.svg` files render as visual previews. Use this for mockups and visualizations. - **No append mode** — to add content to an existing file, read it first, then write the combined content. Or use `bash` with `>>` redirection. - **Line endings** — use `\n` for newlines. The tool does not convert line endings. - **Encoding** — content is written as UTF-8.

name: team_prompt
docs:
## Purpose

Send a new prompt to a team agent to start a follow-up turn. If the agent is currently idle (finished its previous turn), the prompt is sent immediately. If the agent is still active (streaming/processing), the prompt is **queued** and delivered automatically when the current turn finishes.

This is the standard way to give an agent additional tasks, ask questions, or provide feedback after it completes a turn.

## Parameters

| Name | Type | Required | Description |
|------|------|----------|-------------|
| `session_id` | string | **Yes** | Session ID of the agent to prompt (from `team_list`). |
| `message` | string | **Yes** | The prompt message to send. Treated as a user message that starts a new agent turn. |

## When to Use

- **Follow-up tasks** — assigning additional work after the agent completes its initial task.
- **Requesting changes** — asking an agent to revise its work based on review feedback.
- **Status checks** — asking an agent what it has accomplished and whether it's blocked.
- **Providing information** — giving an agent additional context or answers to questions it raised.
- **Queuing work** — sending a prompt to a busy agent so it picks up the task when its current turn ends.

## When NOT to Use

- **Agent is actively going wrong** — use `team_steer` for urgent mid-turn corrections. `team_prompt` waits for the current turn to finish.
- **Agent should be terminated** — use `team_dismiss` to remove agents you no longer need.

## Examples

```
# Give a coder a follow-up task
team_prompt(session_id="abc123", message="Good work on the API endpoints. Now add input validation — tool names should be alphanumeric + hyphens only, max 50 chars. Return 400 for invalid input.")

# Ask for a status update
team_prompt(session_id="abc123", message="What's your current status? Have you finished the implementation? Any blockers?")

# Send revision feedback from a review
team_prompt(session_id="abc123", message="The reviewer found an issue: the PUT endpoint doesn't validate the request body. Add schema validation and return 400 for malformed requests. See the review-findings artifact for details.")

# Queue a task for a busy agent
team_prompt(session_id="abc123", message="When you're done with the current task, also add JSDoc comments to all exported functions in the tool-store module.")
```

## Notes / Gotchas

- **Team lead only** — only team lead sessions can prompt agents.
- **Queued if busy** — if the agent is mid-turn, the prompt is queued and delivered automatically when the turn ends. The agent will process it as its next task.
- **Starts a new turn** — unlike `team_steer` which injects into the current turn, `team_prompt` starts a fresh turn with the message as user input.
- **Check status first** — use `team_list` to see if the agent is active or idle. This helps you decide between `team_prompt` (idle or queue for later) and `team_steer` (redirect now).
- **Be specific** — include file paths, artifact references, and clear acceptance criteria. The agent may have compacted earlier context and lost details.

name: browser_wait
docs:
## Purpose

Wait for a DOM element matching a CSS selector to become **visible** on the page. Polls until the element exists in the DOM and is visible (not hidden by CSS), or the timeout expires. Essential for handling asynchronous content loading, page transitions, and dynamic UI updates.

## Parameters

| Name | Type | Required | Description |
|------|------|----------|-------------|
| `selector` | string | **Yes** | CSS selector of the element to wait for. |
| `timeout` | number | No | Maximum time to wait in **milliseconds**. Default: **10000** (10 seconds). |

## When to Use

- **After navigation** — waiting for the new page's content to render (especially in SPAs where the URL changes but content loads asynchronously).
- **Before clicking dynamic elements** — ensuring a button, link, or menu item is visible before attempting to click it.
- **After triggering async operations** — waiting for loading spinners to disappear, results to appear, modals to open.
- **Form submission results** — waiting for success/error messages after submitting a form.

## When NOT to Use

- **For static pages** — if the page is server-rendered and fully loaded after `browser_navigate`, no wait is needed.
- **For time-based delays** — don't use `browser_wait` as a sleep. If you need a fixed delay, use `browser_eval` with `new Promise(r => setTimeout(r, 1000))`.
- **When the element will never appear** — if the condition can't be met, you'll just waste time hitting the timeout.

## Examples

```
# Wait for a dashboard to load after login
browser_click(selector="button[type='submit']")
browser_wait(selector=".dashboard-content")

# Wait for search results after typing
browser_type(selector="input[name='search']", text="typescript")
browser_wait(selector=".search-results li")

# Wait for a modal dialog to open (5 second timeout)
browser_click(selector=".open-settings")
browser_wait(selector="dialog[open]", timeout=5000)

# Wait for a loading spinner to disappear (wait for content instead)
browser_wait(selector=".data-table tbody tr")

# Wait for an error message with short timeout (3 seconds)
browser_wait(selector=".validation-error", timeout=3000)

# Long wait for slow API response (30 seconds)
browser_wait(selector=".api-results", timeout=30000)
```

## Notes / Gotchas

- **Throws on timeout** — if the element doesn't become visible within the timeout period, the tool returns an error. Handle this case in your workflow.
- **Default 10 seconds (10000ms)** — sufficient for most async loads. Increase for slow API calls or complex page renders. Decrease for quick checks where you want to fail fast.
- **Waits for visibility, not just DOM presence** — the element must exist in the DOM AND be visible (not hidden with `display: none`, `visibility: hidden`, or zero dimensions). This uses Playwright's `state: "visible"` internally.
- **Timeout is in milliseconds** — pass `5000` for 5 seconds, `30000` for 30 seconds. Do NOT pass `5` or `30` — those are 5ms and 30ms, effectively instant.
- **Use before `browser_click`** — this is the most common pattern: wait for the element to be visible, then click it. Clicking without waiting on dynamic content is a race condition.
- **Polling-based** — the tool polls the DOM at intervals, so there may be a slight delay between the element appearing and the tool detecting it.

name: edit
docs:
## Purpose

Perform a precise, surgical text replacement in a file. Finds the exact `oldText` string in the file and replaces it with `newText`. The match must be character-perfect — including whitespace, indentation, and newlines.

This is the safest way to modify existing files because it only changes the targeted text while preserving everything else. Unlike `write`, which replaces the entire file, `edit` touches only what you specify.

Only the **first occurrence** of `oldText` is replaced. If the text appears multiple times, include enough surrounding context to make your match unique.

## Parameters

| Name | Type | Required | Description |
|------|------|----------|-------------|
| `path` | string | **Yes** | Path to the file to edit. Must be an existing file. |
| `oldText` | string | **Yes** | Exact text to find in the file. Must match character-for-character including all whitespace, indentation, and newlines. |
| `newText` | string | **Yes** | Replacement text. Can be empty string to delete the matched text. |

## When to Use

- **Fixing bugs** — change a specific line or expression without touching the rest of the file.
- **Adding imports** — insert a new import line next to existing imports.
- **Modifying function signatures** — change parameters, return types, or function names.
- **Updating configuration values** — change a specific setting in a config file.
- **Any targeted change** — when you know exactly what text to find and what to replace it with.

## When NOT to Use

- **Creating new files** — use `write` instead; `edit` requires the file to already exist.
- **Rewriting most of a file** — if you're changing more than ~50% of the content, `write` is cleaner and less error-prone.
- **When you don't know the exact current text** — always `read` the file first to get the precise text. Guessing the indentation or exact formatting will cause the match to fail.
- **Multi-occurrence replacements** — `edit` only replaces the first match. For global find-and-replace across a file, use `bash` with `sed` or a script.

## Examples

```
# Add an import to an existing import block
edit(path="src/server/server.ts",
  oldText="import { Router } from 'express';",
  newText="import { Router } from 'express';\nimport { ToolStore } from './agent/tool-store.js';")

# Fix a bug — wrong comparison operator
edit(path="src/utils/validator.ts",
  oldText="if (count = 0) {",
  newText="if (count === 0) {")

# Change a function's return type
edit(path="src/api/handler.ts",
  oldText="function getUser(id: string): User {",
  newText="function getUser(id: string): User | null {")

# Update a config value
edit(path="package.json",
  oldText='"version": "1.2.3"',
  newText='"version": "1.3.0"')

# Delete a block of code (replace with empty)
edit(path="src/app.ts",
  oldText="// DEPRECATED: remove after v2\nfunction oldHelper() {\n  return null;\n}\n",
  newText="")
```

## Notes / Gotchas

- **Exact match is non-negotiable** — the `oldText` must match the file content byte-for-byte. Even one space difference causes failure. Always `read` the file first.
- **First occurrence only** — if the same text appears multiple times, only the first is replaced. Include more surrounding context to disambiguate.
- **Whitespace matters** — tabs vs spaces, trailing whitespace, and exact indentation all matter. Copy the text exactly from `read` output.
- **Newlines in parameters** — use `\n` to represent newlines in the oldText and newText strings.
- **Empty newText deletes** — setting `newText` to an empty string effectively deletes the matched text.
- **Verify after editing** — for critical changes, `read` the file afterward to confirm the edit applied correctly.

name: task_create
docs:
## Purpose

Create a new task within the current goal's task board. Tasks track units of work (implementation, code review, testing, bug fixes, refactoring) with dependencies between them. Each task has a lifecycle: `todo` → `in-progress` → `complete` (or `blocked`/`skipped`).

Tasks are the primary coordination mechanism in team workflows. The team lead creates tasks, assigns them to agents, and tracks progress.

**Artifact enforcement**: The server checks artifact requirements before allowing task creation. For example, `implementation` tasks require a `design-doc` artifact to exist. If the required artifact is missing, the tool returns a **409 Conflict** error explaining what's needed.

## Parameters

| Name | Type | Required | Description |
|------|------|----------|-------------|
| `title` | string | **Yes** | Short, descriptive task title. Should be clear enough to understand the task at a glance. |
| `type` | string | **Yes** | Task type. One of: `implementation`, `code-review`, `testing`, `bug-fix`, `refactor`, `custom`. Determines artifact requirements and workflow ordering. |
| `spec` | string | No | Detailed specification for the task. Include requirements, file paths, acceptance criteria, and any relevant context. |
| `depends_on` | string[] | No | Array of task IDs that must be completed before this task can start. Creates explicit dependency ordering. |
| `parent_task_id` | string | No | ID of a parent task. Links this task as a sub-task of the parent, creating a hierarchical task structure. |

## When to Use

- **Planning a goal's work** — break down the goal into tasks before spawning agents.
- **Tracking implementation work** — create implementation tasks for each distinct feature or component.
- **Ordering dependencies** — ensure review happens after implementation, testing after review.
- **Adding tasks mid-flight** — create new tasks as the need emerges (e.g., bug-fix tasks discovered during review).
- **Sub-tasks** — break a large task into smaller sub-tasks using `parent_task_id`.

## When NOT to Use

- **Without a goal** — tasks belong to goals. Create a goal first.
- **Without required artifacts** — `implementation` tasks need a `design-doc`. Create the artifact first.

## Examples

```
# Create an implementation task
task_create(title="Implement tool metadata REST API", type="implementation",
  spec="Create GET/PUT /api/tools/:name endpoints in server.ts. GET returns tool detail including docs field. PUT accepts {description?, group?, docs?} and persists via ToolStore. Return 404 for unknown tools.")

# Create a testing task that depends on implementation
task_create(title="Write E2E tests for tool API", type="testing",
  depends_on=["impl-task-id-123"],
  spec="Cover: GET /api/tools (list), GET /api/tools/:name (detail), PUT /api/tools/:name (update), 404 for unknown. Use patterns from tests/e2e/.")

# Create a sub-task under a parent task
task_create(title="Implement GET /api/tools/:name", type="implementation",
  parent_task_id="parent-task-id-456",
  spec="Implement the GET endpoint only. Return full tool detail including docs field.")

# Create a bug-fix task discovered during review
task_create(title="Fix missing input validation on PUT /api/tools", type="bug-fix",
  spec="The PUT endpoint accepts any body without validation. Add schema checking: description must be string, group must be from allowed list, docs must be string.")
```

## Notes / Gotchas

- **409 Conflict** — if a required artifact is missing, the server rejects the request and tells you which artifact to create first. For `implementation` tasks, you need a `design-doc` artifact.
- **Tasks start as `todo`** — use `task_update` to transition them to `in-progress` and assign to agents.
- **Dependencies are advisory** — the system tracks them but doesn't enforce ordering. It's the team lead's responsibility to respect dependencies when assigning work.
- **Type matters** — the `type` field determines which artifact requirements apply and helps organize the task board.
- **Parent tasks** — use `parent_task_id` to create hierarchical task structures. The parent task ID must refer to an existing task.

name: bash
docs:
## Purpose

Execute an arbitrary bash command in the session's working directory. Returns the combined stdout and stderr output. This is the general-purpose tool for any operation that doesn't have a dedicated tool — running builds, tests, git commands, file searches, system commands, and more.

The command runs synchronously and blocks until completion (or timeout). Output is captured and truncated to the **last 2000 lines or 50KB** (whichever is hit first). If truncation occurs, the full output is saved to a temp file whose path is reported.

The exit code is reported alongside the output, so you can detect command failures.

## Parameters

| Name | Type | Required | Description |
|------|------|----------|-------------|
| `command` | string | **Yes** | The bash command to execute. Can be a simple command, a pipeline, or multiple commands joined with `&&`, `;`, or `|`. |
| `timeout` | number | No | Maximum execution time in seconds. If the command exceeds this, it is killed. No default — commands run until completion. |

## When to Use

- **Running builds and tests** — `npm run build`, `npm test`, `npx playwright test`
- **Git operations** — `git status`, `git diff`, `git log`, `git commit`, `git push`
- **Searching codebases** — `rg 'pattern' src/` (ripgrep), `grep -rn 'pattern' .`
- **File system operations** — `ls`, `find`, `mkdir`, `cp`, `mv`, `rm`
- **Checking system state** — `node --version`, `which python`, `netstat`
- **Running scripts** — `node scripts/migrate.mjs`, `bash setup.sh`
- **Installing packages** — `npm install`, `pip install`
- **Piping and chaining** — complex multi-step operations that combine tools

## When NOT to Use

- **Reading files to examine content** — use `read` instead. It handles pagination, image detection, and encoding properly.
- **Writing or editing files** — use `write` or `edit` for reliable, trackable file modifications.
- **Starting background servers or long-running processes** — the tool waits for all stdout/stderr pipes to close. A backgrounded process (`node server.js &`) that inherits these pipes causes the bash tool to **hang indefinitely** and can crash the agent session.
- **Simple web requests for reading docs** — use `web_fetch` which extracts readable text automatically.

## Examples

```
# Type-check the project before committing
bash(command="npm run check")

# Run E2E tests with filtered output
bash(command="npm run build:server && npx playwright test --config playwright-e2e.config.ts --reporter=json 2>/dev/null | node scripts/test-filter.mjs")

# Search for all usages of a function across the codebase
bash(command="rg 'handleApiRoute' src/ --type ts -n")

# Git workflow: check status, stage, and commit
bash(command="git add -A && git status && git commit -m 'feat: add tool documentation endpoint'")

# Check what's on a branch compared to master
bash(command="git log --oneline master..HEAD")

# Find all TypeScript files modified in the last commit
bash(command="git diff --name-only HEAD~1 -- '*.ts'")

# Run a specific test file with timeout
bash(command="npx playwright test tests/e2e/tools-api.spec.ts --reporter=json 2>/dev/null | node scripts/test-filter.mjs", timeout=120)
```

## Notes / Gotchas

- **Output truncation** — only the last 2000 lines or 50KB are returned. For large outputs, redirect to a file (`cmd > output.txt`) and `read` it, or pipe through `tail`/`head`.
- **No background processes** — `node server.js &`, `nohup`, or any backgrounded command that inherits stdout/stderr will cause the tool to hang forever. Use Playwright's `webServer` config for test servers.
- **Working directory** — commands run in the session's working directory. Use `cd path &&` within the command if you need to run from a different directory, but prefer staying in the session directory.
- **Exit code** — reported alongside output. Check it to detect failures in chained commands.
- **Platform** — runs in Git Bash on Windows. Most Unix commands work, but some platform-specific tools may differ. `$TEMP` is available for temp files (not `/tmp`).
- **Timeout** — if set, the command is killed after the specified seconds. Use for commands that might hang (network operations, potentially infinite loops).
- **Pipes and redirects** — full bash syntax is supported including pipes (`|`), redirects (`>`, `>>`, `2>&1`), subshells, and command substitution (`$()`).

name: delegate
docs:
## Purpose

Spawn an isolated agent subprocess to execute a task independently. The delegate agent gets full tool access (bash, read, write, edit, web search, browser tools) but receives **only the instructions you provide** — it has no access to the current conversation history, messages, or context.

The delegate runs as a completely separate process and blocks the caller until it finishes. Its final output is returned as the result. This enables two key patterns: **context isolation** (the delegate can't see sensitive conversation content) and **mass parallelism** (multiple delegates run concurrently).

The `parallel` parameter accepts an array of instruction objects to spawn multiple delegates simultaneously. All run concurrently and the tool returns when all complete.

## Parameters

| Name | Type | Required | Description |
|------|------|----------|-------------|
| `instructions` | string | Optional (provide either `instructions` or `parallel`) | Detailed task instructions for a single delegate. Must be self-contained — the delegate has no other context. |
| `context` | object | No | Key-value pairs of additional context passed to the delegate (e.g., file paths, error messages, configuration). |
| `parallel` | array | Optional (provide either `instructions` or `parallel`) | Array of `{ instructions: string, context?: object }` objects. Each spawns an independent delegate. All run concurrently. |
| `timeout_minutes` | number | No | Maximum execution time in minutes. Default: **10 minutes**. |

## When to Use

- **Context isolation** — when the delegate must NOT see the current conversation (e.g., independent code review, unbiased analysis).
- **Mass parallelism** — 3 or more independent sub-tasks that benefit from concurrent execution (e.g., reviewing multiple modules, generating docs for multiple components, running analyses across different areas).

## When NOT to Use

- **Single tasks you can do yourself** — if there's no need for isolation or parallelism, do the work inline. Delegating adds significant overhead (spawning a new agent process) for zero benefit.
- **Reading files** — never delegate just to read files. `read` is instant; delegating it wastes a full agent spawn.
- **Parallelizing file reads** — even for many files, read them yourself with parallel `read` calls in one message. Delegates are slower, not faster, for this.
- **Simple sequential tasks** — "the task is complex" or "the task is long" are NOT valid reasons to delegate. You can handle complex, long tasks directly.

## Examples

```
# Context-isolated code review (single delegate with instructions)
delegate(instructions="Review the changes in src/server/auth/ for security vulnerabilities. The branch has modifications to token validation, rate limiting, and TLS setup. Check for: injection attacks, timing attacks, insecure defaults, missing validation. Report findings as structured markdown with severity levels.")

# Parallel analysis of independent modules
delegate(parallel=[
  { instructions: "Analyze src/server/ for error handling gaps. Check every async function for missing try/catch, unhandled promise rejections, and error propagation. Report as markdown." },
  { instructions: "Analyze src/ui/ for accessibility issues. Check for missing ARIA labels, keyboard navigation, color contrast, and screen reader compatibility. Report as markdown." },
  { instructions: "Analyze src/app/ for memory leak risks. Check for unremoved event listeners, unclosed connections, growing arrays, and retained references. Report as markdown." }
])

# Delegate with context
delegate(
  instructions="Fix the failing E2E test. The test expects a 200 response but gets 404. Check the API route handler and the test setup.",
  context={ testFile: "tests/e2e/tools-api.spec.ts", errorLine: "42", endpoint: "/api/tools/bash" }
)

# Parallel documentation generation
delegate(parallel=[
  { instructions: "Write comprehensive JSDoc comments for all exported functions in src/server/agent/session-manager.ts" },
  { instructions: "Write comprehensive JSDoc comments for all exported functions in src/server/agent/goal-manager.ts" },
  { instructions: "Write comprehensive JSDoc comments for all exported functions in src/server/agent/task-manager.ts" }
])
```

## Notes / Gotchas

- **Instructions must be self-contained** — the delegate sees ONLY what you put in `instructions` and `context`. It has no conversation history, no knowledge of prior decisions, no awareness of other delegates. Include all necessary file paths, requirements, and context.
- **Significant overhead** — each delegate spawns an entire agent process. Only delegate when the isolation or parallelism benefit justifies this cost.
- **Provide `instructions` or `parallel`, not both** — use `instructions` for a single delegate, `parallel` for multiple concurrent delegates. Neither is strictly required at the schema level, but one must be provided at runtime.
- **10-minute default timeout** — delegates that exceed the timeout are killed. Increase `timeout_minutes` for longer tasks.
- **Blocking** — the `delegate` tool blocks until all delegates finish. Plan accordingly for long-running delegates.
- **No inter-delegate communication** — parallel delegates cannot talk to each other. They run independently and their results are collected when all finish.
- **Full tool access** — delegates can read, write, edit, run bash, search the web, and use browser tools. They can make changes to the filesystem.
- **File conflicts in parallel** — if multiple parallel delegates edit the same file, the last write wins. Design parallel tasks to work on independent files.

name: task_list
docs:
## Purpose

List all tasks for the current goal with their full metadata: state, type, assignment, dependencies, spec, and results. This is the dashboard view of the goal's task board.

## Parameters

None.

## Returns

An array of task objects, each containing:
- `id` — unique task identifier
- `title` — short task description
- `type` — task type (implementation, code-review, testing, bug-fix, refactor, custom)
- `state` — current state: `todo`, `in-progress`, `blocked`, `complete`, or `skipped`
- `assignedSessionId` — session ID of the assigned agent (null if unassigned)
- `dependsOn` — array of task IDs this task depends on
- `spec` — detailed task specification
- `resultSummary` — completion summary (populated when task is completed)
- `commitSha` — associated git commit (if set)
- `parentTaskId` — ID of the parent task (null if top-level)
- `completedAt` — timestamp when the task was completed (null if not yet complete)

## When to Use

- **Planning** — review existing tasks before creating new ones.
- **Progress tracking** — check which tasks are complete, in-progress, or blocked.
- **Assignment decisions** — find unassigned tasks to give to available agents.
- **Dependency checking** — verify prerequisite tasks are complete before starting dependent work.
- **Completion verification** — confirm all tasks are done before completing the goal.

## When NOT to Use

- This tool has no restrictions — use it freely and frequently.

## Examples

```
# List all tasks to check progress
task_list()

# Typical workflow: list tasks, find unassigned ones, assign them
# 1. task_list()  → see which tasks are todo, check assignedSessionId for unassigned
# 2. team_spawn(role="coder", task="...")  → spawn an agent
# 3. task_update(task_id="...", assigned_to="session-id", state="in-progress")
```

## Notes / Gotchas

- **Lightweight and fast** — no parameters, instant response. Call frequently to stay informed.
- **All tasks returned** — returns every task regardless of state. Filter by state in your logic if needed.
- **Check before creating** — always list existing tasks before creating new ones to avoid duplicates.
- **Field name** — the assignment field is `assignedSessionId`, not `assignedTo`.

name: web_search
docs:
## Purpose

Search the web using DuckDuckGo and return a list of results with titles, URLs, and text snippets. This is a fast, zero-configuration web search — no API key needed.

Use this as the starting point for any web research. It returns enough information in the snippets to often answer questions directly, and provides URLs for deeper reading via `web_fetch`.

## How It Works

This tool works by scraping DuckDuckGo's HTML search results page via curl — it does not use an official search API. Results (titles, URLs, snippets) are extracted from the HTML response. This means:

- **No API key needed** — searches are anonymous, no configuration required.
- **Rate limiting** — DuckDuckGo may occasionally rate-limit requests if many searches are made in quick succession. If results come back empty, wait a moment and retry.
- **Formatting inconsistencies** — since results are parsed from HTML, occasional formatting artifacts (extra whitespace, truncated snippets) may appear.

## Parameters

| Name | Type | Required | Description |
|------|------|----------|-------------|
| `query` | string | **Yes** | The search query. Use natural language or keyword-style queries. More specific queries yield better results. |
| `maxResults` | number | No | Maximum number of results to return. Range: 1-20. Default: 10. |

## When to Use

- **Looking up documentation** — API references, library docs, language features.
- **Debugging errors** — paste error messages or codes to find solutions.
- **Checking current information** — version numbers, release dates, deprecation notices.
- **Finding best practices** — design patterns, architecture guidance, security recommendations.
- **Researching before implementing** — understanding how others have solved similar problems.

## When NOT to Use

- **When you already know the URL** — use `web_fetch` directly to read a known page.
- **For internal/local information** — use `read` or `bash` to examine local files and project state.
- **For real-time data** — search results may be cached/delayed. DuckDuckGo doesn't guarantee real-time freshness.

## Examples

```
# Look up API documentation
web_search(query="Node.js crypto createHash API reference")

# Debug an error message
web_search(query="TypeScript error TS2345 argument of type string not assignable")

# Research a design pattern
web_search(query="WebSocket reconnection with exponential backoff best practices")

# Find library-specific guidance
web_search(query="Lit web components reactive controller lifecycle")

# Check compatibility
web_search(query="CSS container queries browser support 2024")
```

## Notes / Gotchas

- **HTML scraping, not an API** — results are extracted from DuckDuckGo's HTML page via curl. There is no official API involved, so results may occasionally have formatting inconsistencies or be rate-limited.
- **Search from multiple angles** — launch 3-5 parallel searches with different phrasings in a single message for better coverage. This is much faster than sequential searches.
- **Follow up with `web_fetch`** — snippets are brief. Fetch the full page for detailed information.
- **Cross-reference claims** — verify important facts appear in at least two independent sources.
- **No authentication** — searches are anonymous via DuckDuckGo. No API key configuration needed.
- **Result quality** — DuckDuckGo results may differ from Google. If results are sparse, try rephrasing the query.
- **Max 20 results** — even with `maxResults=20`, DuckDuckGo may return fewer if there aren't enough relevant results.

name: team_spawn
docs:
## Purpose

Spawn a new agent with a specific role (coder, reviewer, tester, or custom) as part of a goal's team. The agent gets its own isolated **git worktree** branched from the goal branch, ensuring its file changes don't interfere with other agents until explicitly merged.

The spawned agent receives the role's system prompt (defining its capabilities and restrictions), the role's tool allowlist, and the provided task as its first user message. The agent then works independently on that task.

Returns the new agent's session ID and worktree path, which you use for subsequent `team_steer`, `team_prompt`, and `team_dismiss` calls.

## Parameters

| Name | Type | Required | Description |
|------|------|----------|-------------|
| `role` | string | **Yes** | The role to assign. Built-in roles: `coder`, `reviewer`, `tester`. Custom roles defined via the role management page also work. |
| `task` | string | **Yes** | Task description sent as the agent's first user prompt. Should be specific and self-contained — include references to artifacts, file paths, and acceptance criteria. |

## When to Use

- **Implementing features** — spawn a `coder` with a clear task description referencing the design doc.
- **Code review** — spawn a `reviewer` to review changes on a specific branch.
- **Writing tests** — spawn a `tester` to create test suites for new functionality.
- **Parallel work** — spawn multiple agents with different roles to work concurrently on independent tasks.

## When NOT to Use

- **Simple one-off tasks** — if the task is small enough to do yourself, don't spawn an agent.
- **Without a goal** — team tools require an active goal context.
- **Without a design doc** — implementation tasks require a `design-doc` artifact to exist first (enforced by artifact requirements).

## Examples

```
# Spawn a coder for a feature implementation
team_spawn(role="coder", task="Implement the tool metadata REST API endpoints (GET/PUT /api/tools/:name) as described in the design doc. Check artifact_list and artifact_get for the design document. Run npm run check before committing.")

# Spawn a reviewer to review changes
team_spawn(role="reviewer", task="Review the changes on the goal branch against master. Focus on correctness, error handling, and API contract consistency. Create a review-findings artifact with your results.")

# Spawn a tester for E2E test coverage
team_spawn(role="tester", task="Write E2E tests for the new /api/tools endpoints. Cover: GET list, GET single, PUT update, 404 for unknown tools. Follow patterns in tests/e2e/. Run tests and ensure they pass.")
```

## Notes / Gotchas

- **Team lead only** — only sessions with team lead status can spawn agents.
- **Isolated worktrees** — each agent works in a separate git worktree. Changes must be merged back to the goal branch (the agent's system prompt instructs them how).
- **Artifact gates** — spawning a coder for an implementation task may fail with 409 if the required `design-doc` artifact doesn't exist yet.
- **Agent independence** — spawned agents work autonomously. Use `team_list` to monitor progress, `team_steer` for mid-turn corrections, and `team_prompt` for follow-up tasks.
- **Resource usage** — each agent is a full agent process with its own context window. Spawn only as many as needed.
- **Worktree cleanup** — worktrees are cleaned up when the agent is dismissed via `team_dismiss` or `team_complete`.

name: task_update
docs:
## Purpose

Update one or more fields on an existing task in a single atomic call. This is the primary tool for managing task lifecycle: transitioning states, assigning agents, recording results, and linking commits.

Multiple fields can be updated simultaneously — for example, assigning an agent and starting the task in one call.

## Parameters

| Name | Type | Required | Description |
|------|------|----------|-------------|
| `task_id` | string | **Yes** | The unique ID of the task to update. Get this from `task_list`. |
| `title` | string | No | Updated task title. |
| `spec` | string | No | Updated task specification. |
| `result_summary` | string | No | Summary of what was accomplished. Best practice: always set this when marking a task complete. |
| `commit_sha` | string | No | Git commit SHA associated with the task's work. |
| `assigned_to` | string | No | Session ID to assign the task to. Get the session ID from `team_spawn` or `team_list`. |
| `state` | string | No | New state. See valid transitions below. |
| `depends_on` | string[] | No | Array of task IDs this task depends on. Replaces existing dependencies. |

## Valid State Transitions

State transitions are **enforced** — invalid transitions are rejected. The valid transitions are:

- `todo` → `in-progress`
- `todo` → `skipped`
- `in-progress` → `complete`
- `in-progress` → `blocked`
- `in-progress` → `skipped`
- `blocked` → `in-progress`
- `blocked` → `skipped`

For example, you cannot go from `complete` → `in-progress` or from `todo` → `complete` directly.

## When to Use

- **Starting a task** — set `state="in-progress"` and `assigned_to` when an agent begins work.
- **Completing a task** — set `state="complete"` and `result_summary` when work is done and verified.
- **Blocking a task** — set `state="blocked"` and `result_summary` explaining the blocker.
- **Recording results** — update `result_summary` and `commit_sha` with completion details.
- **Reassigning** — change `assigned_to` if a task needs to move to a different agent.
- **Updating dependencies** — change `depends_on` to add or modify task ordering.

## When NOT to Use

- **Creating tasks** — use `task_create` for new tasks.
- **Listing tasks** — use `task_list` to see all tasks.

## Examples

```
# Assign a task to an agent and start it
task_update(task_id="abc123", assigned_to="session-456", state="in-progress")

# Mark a task complete with summary and commit
task_update(task_id="abc123", state="complete",
  result_summary="Implemented GET/PUT /api/tools/:name endpoints. All E2E tests pass. Merged to goal branch.",
  commit_sha="a1b2c3d4")

# Mark a task as blocked
task_update(task_id="abc123", state="blocked",
  result_summary="Blocked: the ToolStore module hasn't been implemented yet. Depends on task def456.")

# Skip a task that's no longer needed
task_update(task_id="abc123", state="skipped",
  result_summary="Skipped: requirements changed, this endpoint is no longer needed.")

# Update dependencies
task_update(task_id="abc123", depends_on=["task-1", "task-2"])
```

## Notes / Gotchas

- **Atomic updates** — all fields provided in a single call are updated atomically. No partial failures.
- **Always include `result_summary` on completion** — this is the primary way to communicate what was accomplished. The team lead and other agents rely on it.
- **State transitions are enforced** — invalid transitions (e.g., `complete` → `in-progress`) are rejected with an error. Follow the valid transition graph.
- **`assigned_to` is a session ID** — get it from `team_spawn` (return value) or `team_list` (agent list).
- **Multiple updates at once** — you can set state, assignment, and result in a single call. This is preferred over multiple separate calls.

name: web_fetch
docs:
## Purpose

Fetch a web page by URL and extract readable text content. Uses curl internally for speed, then strips HTML boilerplate (navigation, ads, scripts) to return clean, readable text.

This is significantly faster than the browser tools and should be your default choice for reading web pages that don't require JavaScript rendering. Use it to read documentation, articles, README files, API references, and any other text-heavy page.

## Parameters

| Name | Type | Required | Description |
|------|------|----------|-------------|
| `url` | string | **Yes** | The URL to fetch. Must be a full URL including protocol (https://...). |
| `maxLength` | number | No | Maximum characters of extracted text to return. Default: 20,000. Increase for long documentation pages. |

## When to Use

- **Reading documentation pages** found via `web_search`.
- **Fetching README files** from GitHub or other repositories.
- **Reading API references** and technical specifications.
- **Checking changelogs** and release notes.
- **Any static or server-rendered web page** where you need the text content.

## When NOT to Use

- **JavaScript-heavy SPAs** — if the page content is rendered client-side (React, Vue, Angular SPAs), the extracted text will be incomplete. Use `browser_navigate` + `browser_eval` instead.
- **Pages requiring authentication** — `web_fetch` doesn't carry browser cookies or sessions. Use browser tools for authenticated pages.
- **Downloading binary files** — use `bash` with `curl -o` for downloading binaries, images, or archives.
- **When you need to interact with the page** — clicking, typing, scrolling require browser tools.

## Examples

```
# Read API documentation
web_fetch(url="https://nodejs.org/api/crypto.html")

# Read a GitHub README
web_fetch(url="https://github.com/lit/lit/blob/main/README.md")

# Fetch a long documentation page with increased limit
web_fetch(url="https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/2/types-from-types.html", maxLength=50000)

# Read a Stack Overflow answer
web_fetch(url="https://stackoverflow.com/questions/12345678/how-to-handle-websocket-reconnection")

# Check a package's npm page
web_fetch(url="https://www.npmjs.com/package/ws")
```

## Notes / Gotchas

- **Default 20K character limit** — long documentation pages may be truncated. Set `maxLength` higher (e.g., 50000) for comprehensive docs.
- **Text extraction is heuristic** — it strips navigation, sidebars, and boilerplate, but isn't perfect. Some pages may have missing or garbled content.
- **No JavaScript execution** — pages that require JS to render content (SPAs, lazy-loaded content) will return incomplete or empty text. Use browser tools for these.
- **Speed** — much faster than browser tools since it's just a curl + text extraction. Prefer this for any page that works without JS.
- **Parallel fetches** — launch multiple `web_fetch` calls in a single message to read several pages concurrently.
- **Redirects** — follows HTTP redirects automatically.
- **Encoding** — handles common encodings (UTF-8, Latin-1, etc.) automatically.

name: team_list
docs:
## Purpose

List all agents currently in the team, showing their role, status, assigned task, and session ID. This is the primary tool for monitoring team progress and deciding next actions (steer, prompt, dismiss, or wait).

## Parameters

None.

## Returns

An array of agent objects, each containing:
- `sessionId` — unique session identifier (used for steer/prompt/dismiss)
- `role` — the agent's assigned role (coder, reviewer, tester, or custom)
- `status` — current session status (see Status Values below)
- `task` — the original task description
- `name` — the agent's generated display name

### Status Values

The `status` field is one of these exact string values:

| Status | Meaning |
|--------|----------|
| `starting` | Agent process is launching but not yet ready. |
| `streaming` | Agent is actively processing — generating output or executing tools. Use `team_steer` to redirect. |
| `idle` | Agent has finished its current turn and is waiting. Use `team_prompt` to assign more work. |
| `terminated` | Agent process has been shut down. Cannot be steered or prompted. |

## When to Use

- **Monitoring progress** — check which agents are still working, which are idle, which are blocked.
- **Before steering** — verify an agent's status is `streaming` before sending a steer message.
- **Before prompting** — confirm an agent's status is `idle` before sending a follow-up prompt.
- **Planning dismissals** — identify which agents have finished and can be dismissed.
- **Before spawning** — check how many agents are already active to avoid over-provisioning.

## When NOT to Use

- This tool has no restrictions — use it freely whenever you need team status.

## Examples

```
# Check on all team agents
team_list()
# Returns: [{ sessionId: "abc123", role: "coder", status: "streaming", task: "Implement...", name: "swift-falcon" }, ...]
```

## Notes / Gotchas

- **Team lead only** — only available to team lead sessions.
- **Lightweight** — no parameters, fast response. Call frequently to stay informed about team state.
- **Status values** — `streaming` means the agent is actively processing. `idle` means it has finished its current turn and is waiting. `starting` means the process is launching. `terminated` means the agent has been shut down. Check status before choosing between `team_steer` (streaming) and `team_prompt` (idle).

name: browser_navigate
docs:
## Purpose

Navigate a headless Chromium browser to a specified URL. If no browser is currently running, one is launched automatically. The browser session persists across all browser tool calls — cookies, localStorage, session state, and page context carry over between navigations.

This is the entry point for all browser-based interactions. After navigating, use `browser_screenshot`, `browser_click`, `browser_type`, `browser_eval`, and `browser_wait` to interact with the page.

## Parameters

| Name | Type | Required | Description |
|------|------|----------|-------------|
| `url` | string | **Yes** | The URL to navigate to. Must include protocol (https://, http://, file://). |

## When to Use

- **JavaScript-rendered pages** — SPAs (React, Vue, Angular) that require JS to display content.
- **Interactive testing** — verifying UI behavior by clicking, typing, and observing results.
- **Pages requiring authentication** — login flows where you need to type credentials and submit forms.
- **Local development servers** — checking your own app running on localhost.
- **Visual verification** — navigating to a page to take a screenshot for UI review.

## When NOT to Use

- **Static documentation and articles** — use `web_fetch` instead. It's 10x faster and extracts clean text.
- **Simple API calls** — use `bash` with `curl` for REST API testing.
- **Searching the web** — use `web_search` for DuckDuckGo results.
- **Reading known URLs with static content** — `web_fetch` is faster and returns cleaner text.

## Examples

```
# Navigate to a local dev server to check UI
browser_navigate(url="https://localhost:5173")

# Open a JavaScript-heavy documentation site
browser_navigate(url="https://react.dev/reference/react/useState")

# Navigate to a page requiring client-side rendering
browser_navigate(url="https://app.example.com/dashboard")

# Open a local HTML fixture for testing
browser_navigate(url="file:///C:/Users/user/project/tests/fixtures/test.html")
```

## Notes / Gotchas

- **Slower than `web_fetch`** — the browser has significant overhead (launch, rendering, JS execution). Only use when you genuinely need a full browser.
- **Persistent session** — the browser stays open and state carries over. Navigating to a new URL preserves cookies and localStorage from previous pages on the same origin.
- **Self-signed certs** — the headless browser may reject self-signed HTTPS certificates. For local dev servers, use `http://` or configure the browser to accept insecure certs.
- **Follows redirects** — HTTP redirects and JS-based redirects are followed automatically.
- **Page load wait** — the tool waits for the `DOMContentLoaded` event before returning, which fires when HTML is parsed but before images, stylesheets, and subframes finish loading. For content that loads asynchronously after DOMContentLoaded, follow up with `browser_wait`.

name: team_dismiss
docs:
## Purpose

Terminate a specific team agent and clean up its resources. This kills the agent's session, removes its git worktree, and frees the associated system resources. The agent is permanently removed from the team.

## Parameters

| Name | Type | Required | Description |
|------|------|----------|-------------|
| `session_id` | string | **Yes** | The session ID of the agent to dismiss. Get this from `team_list`. |

## When to Use

- **Agent completed its task** — the agent has finished, merged its work, and is idle.
- **Agent is stuck or broken** — the agent is producing incorrect output or is in an unrecoverable state.
- **Resource management** — freeing up resources by removing agents that are no longer needed.
- **Replacing an agent** — dismiss the old one before spawning a replacement.

## When NOT to Use

- **Agent hasn't merged its work** — ensure the agent has pushed and merged changes to the goal branch first, or the work will be lost with the worktree.
- **Dismissing all agents** — use `team_complete` instead for a clean end-of-goal dismissal.

## Examples

```
# Dismiss a coder that has finished and merged its work
team_dismiss(session_id="abc123-def456-789")

# Dismiss a stuck agent to replace with a new one
team_dismiss(session_id="stuck-agent-session-id")
```

## Notes / Gotchas

- **Team lead only** — only team lead sessions can dismiss agents.
- **Irreversible** — the agent's session is killed and its worktree is deleted. Any uncommitted or unmerged work is lost.
- **Verify merge first** — always confirm the agent has merged its changes back to the goal branch before dismissing. Check with `team_list` or review the git log.
- **Worktree cleanup** — the agent's isolated git worktree directory is removed automatically.

name: team_complete
docs:
## Purpose

End the team workflow by dismissing all role agents and marking the goal as complete. This is the final step in a goal's lifecycle — it cleans up all role agent sessions, removes their git worktrees, and transitions the goal state to `done`.

**Important**: The team lead session is NOT dismissed — only role agents (coders, reviewers, testers) are terminated. The team lead stays active to present a final report and handle any follow-up.

## Parameters

None.

## Returns

Confirmation that all role agents were dismissed and the goal was marked complete.

## When to Use

- **Goal is finished** — all tasks are complete, code is reviewed, tests pass, and everything is merged.
- **Final cleanup** — as the very last action after verifying all work is done.

## When NOT to Use

- **Work is still in progress** — don't complete until all agents have finished, merged, and been verified.
- **Missing artifacts** — goal completion requires a `review-findings` artifact. If it doesn't exist, completion will be blocked.
- **Dismissing a single agent** — use `team_dismiss` for individual agents.

## Examples

```
# Complete the goal after all work is verified
team_complete()
```

## Notes / Gotchas

- **Team lead only** — only team lead sessions can complete goals.
- **Team lead stays active** — unlike `team_dismiss`, this does NOT terminate the team lead session. The lead remains active to summarize results and present a report to the user.
- **Role agents are dismissed** — all coder, reviewer, and tester agents are terminated and their worktrees removed. Ensure all work is merged first.
- **Artifact requirement** — the default goal configuration requires a `review-findings` artifact before completion. Create this artifact (usually from a code review) before calling `team_complete`.
- **Irreversible** — the goal transitions to `done` state. All team resources (except the lead) are cleaned up.
- **Call last** — this should be the very last action in a goal workflow, after all verification is complete.

name: browser_screenshot
docs:
## Purpose

Capture a visual screenshot of the current browser page or a specific element. The screenshot is returned as an image attachment that renders inline in the chat, allowing visual inspection of UI state, layout, and rendering.

## Parameters

| Name | Type | Required | Description |
|------|------|----------|-------------|
| `selector` | string | No | CSS selector to screenshot a specific element instead of the full page. If omitted, captures the entire visible viewport. |
| `savePath` | string | No | File path to save the screenshot as a PNG file on disk. If omitted, the screenshot is only returned as an inline attachment. |
| `fullPage` | boolean | No | When `true`, captures the full scrollable page, not just the visible viewport. Default: **false**. |

## When to Use

- **Visual verification** — confirming UI changes look correct after modifications.
- **Debugging layout issues** — seeing exactly how elements are positioned and styled.
- **Capturing error states** — screenshotting error dialogs, broken layouts, or unexpected UI.
- **Documenting current state** — creating a visual record of how something looks before changing it.
- **Comparing before/after** — taking screenshots before and after a change.
- **Saving screenshots to disk** — use `savePath` to persist screenshots for reports or later comparison.

## When NOT to Use

- **Extracting text content** — use `browser_eval` to get text via DOM queries. Screenshots can't be searched or copied.
- **When no page is loaded** — call `browser_navigate` first.
- **For non-visual checks** — if you just need to verify a value exists, `browser_eval` is faster and more reliable.

## Examples

```
# Screenshot the full page
browser_screenshot()

# Screenshot a specific component
browser_screenshot(selector=".sidebar-navigation")

# Capture the full scrollable page (not just the viewport)
browser_screenshot(fullPage=true)

# Save a screenshot to disk
browser_screenshot(savePath="screenshots/dashboard.png")

# Screenshot a modal dialog
browser_screenshot(selector="dialog[open]")

# Capture full page and save to disk
browser_screenshot(fullPage=true, savePath="screenshots/full-page.png")
```

## Notes / Gotchas

- **Must navigate first** — a page must be loaded via `browser_navigate` before taking screenshots.
- **Viewport vs full page** — by default, only the visible viewport is captured. Use `fullPage: true` to capture everything including content below the fold.
- **Element screenshots** — when using a `selector`, only that element's bounding box is captured. If the selector matches nothing, the tool will error.
- **Hidden elements** — elements with `display: none` or `visibility: hidden` won't appear in screenshots even if selected.
- **Rendered inline** — the screenshot image appears directly in the chat conversation as an attachment.

name: artifact_create
docs:
## Purpose

Create a structured artifact associated with the current goal. Artifacts are formal documents — design docs, test plans, review findings, security analyses — that serve both as process records and as **workflow gates** that control what tasks and actions are allowed.

The artifact is stored with versioning (starting at version 1) and metadata about who created it and when. Artifacts are visible to all agents working on the goal and persist across sessions.

**Workflow enforcement**: Certain artifact types gate downstream actions:
- `design-doc` must exist before `implementation` tasks can be created
- `review-findings` must exist before the goal can be completed via `team_complete`

## Parameters

| Name | Type | Required | Description |
|------|------|----------|-------------|
| `name` | string | **Yes** | Human-readable artifact name (e.g., "Design Document", "Security Review Findings"). |
| `type` | string | **Yes** | Artifact type. One of: `design-doc`, `test-plan`, `review-findings`, `gap-analysis`, `security-findings`, `custom`. |
| `content` | string | **Yes** | The artifact content. Typically markdown for documents, JSON for structured data. |

## When to Use

- **Before implementation** — create a `design-doc` to unblock implementation tasks.
- **After code review** — create `review-findings` to document issues and approve the code.
- **Planning tests** — create a `test-plan` before writing tests.
- **Security analysis** — create `security-findings` to document security review results.
- **Any structured output** — use `custom` for artifacts that don't fit other categories.

## When NOT to Use

- **Updating an existing artifact** — use `artifact_update` instead of creating a duplicate.
- **Informal notes** — artifacts are formal documents. Use chat messages for informal communication.

## Examples

```
# Create a design document to unblock implementation
artifact_create(name="Design Document", type="design-doc",
  content="# Tool Management API\n\n## Overview\nREST API for managing tool metadata...\n\n## Endpoints\n### GET /api/tools/:name\n...\n\n## Data Model\n...")

# Create review findings to unblock goal completion
artifact_create(name="Code Review Findings", type="review-findings",
  content="# Code Review\n\n## Summary\nReviewed 12 files, 450 lines changed.\n\n## Critical Issues\nNone.\n\n## Recommendations\n1. Add input validation to PUT endpoint\n2. Add JSDoc to exported functions\n\n## Verdict\nApproved with minor suggestions.")

# Create a test plan
artifact_create(name="Test Plan", type="test-plan",
  content="# Test Plan\n\n## Scope\nE2E tests for tool management API\n\n## Test Cases\n1. GET /api/tools returns all tools\n2. GET /api/tools/:name returns detail\n...")

# Create security findings
artifact_create(name="Security Review", type="security-findings",
  content="# Security Review\n\n## Findings\n### HIGH: No rate limiting on PUT endpoint\n...\n\n## Recommendations\n...")
```

## Notes / Gotchas

- **Workflow gates are enforced** — trying to create an `implementation` task without a `design-doc` artifact returns 409. Create the design doc first.
- **Content is stored inline** — the full content is persisted in the artifact store. Keep artifacts reasonably sized (markdown docs, not binary data).
- **Version 1** — newly created artifacts start at version 1. Use `artifact_update` to revise (increments version).
- **Visible to all agents** — any agent on the goal can read artifacts via `artifact_list` and `artifact_get`.
- **`producedBy` is automatic** — the creating session's ID is recorded automatically.

name: browser_click
docs:
## Purpose

Click on a DOM element identified by a CSS selector. Simulates a full mouse click (mousedown, mouseup, click events) on the first element matching the selector. Any navigation, form submission, or JavaScript event handlers triggered by the click happen automatically.

## Parameters

| Name | Type | Required | Description |
|------|------|----------|-------------|
| `selector` | string | **Yes** | CSS selector identifying the element to click. If multiple elements match, the first one is clicked. |

## When to Use

- **Submitting forms** — clicking submit buttons after filling in fields with `browser_type`.
- **Navigating within SPAs** — clicking links or navigation items that use client-side routing.
- **Toggling UI elements** — expanding dropdowns, opening modals, toggling switches.
- **Testing interactive features** — verifying that click handlers work as expected.

## When NOT to Use

- **When the element might not exist yet** — call `browser_wait` first to ensure the element is in the DOM.
- **For typing into inputs** — use `browser_type` for text entry.
- **When you need the click result as data** — use `browser_eval` to call click programmatically and capture the return value.

## Examples

```
# Click a submit button
browser_click(selector="button[type='submit']")

# Click a navigation link
browser_click(selector="a[href='/settings']")

# Click by data-testid attribute
browser_click(selector="[data-testid='save-button']")

# Click the first item in a list
browser_click(selector=".search-results li:first-child a")

# Click a dropdown toggle
browser_click(selector=".dropdown-trigger")
```

## Notes / Gotchas

- **Element must be visible and clickable** — hidden elements, elements behind overlays, or elements with `pointer-events: none` may not respond to clicks.
- **First match only** — if the selector matches multiple elements, the first one in DOM order is clicked. Be specific with your selector to target the right element.
- **Navigation happens automatically** — if the click triggers a page navigation, the browser follows it. No need to call `browser_navigate` after.
- **Wait for dynamic elements** — if the element appears after an async operation (API call, animation), use `browser_wait(selector=...)` before clicking.
- **Scroll into view** — the browser typically scrolls the element into view before clicking, but deeply nested or overflow-hidden elements may cause issues.

name: team_steer
docs:
## Purpose

Send a mid-turn redirect message to an agent that is currently active (streaming/processing). The message is injected into the agent's context as a system-level instruction, causing it to change course without waiting for its current turn to finish.

This is the "course correction" tool — use it when you can see an agent heading in the wrong direction and want to redirect it immediately rather than waiting for it to finish and then giving feedback.

## Parameters

| Name | Type | Required | Description |
|------|------|----------|-------------|
| `session_id` | string | **Yes** | Session ID of the agent to steer (from `team_list`). |
| `message` | string | **Yes** | Steering instruction to inject. Should be clear and directive — tell the agent exactly what to do differently. |

## When to Use

- **Agent is going the wrong direction** — it's implementing the wrong approach, editing the wrong files, or misunderstanding the task.
- **New information arrived** — a design doc was updated, a dependency was added, or requirements changed while the agent is working.
- **Preventing wasted work** — catching a mistake early before the agent builds on top of it.

## When NOT to Use

- **Agent is idle** — use `team_prompt` for agents that have finished their turn. `team_steer` fails with an error if the agent is not actively streaming.
- **Routine follow-up tasks** — if the agent finished correctly and you want to assign more work, use `team_prompt`.
- **Non-urgent feedback** — if the agent is on the right track and you just have minor suggestions, wait for it to finish and use `team_prompt`.

## Examples

```
# Redirect an agent that's editing the wrong file
team_steer(session_id="abc123", message="STOP — you're editing server.ts but the route handlers moved to router.ts in the last refactor. Check src/server/router.ts instead.")

# Update an agent with new requirements
team_steer(session_id="abc123", message="The design doc was just updated. The API should return 404 for unknown tools, not 200 with empty object. Check the latest artifact version.")

# Prevent over-engineering
team_steer(session_id="abc123", message="Keep it simple — don't add caching yet. Just implement the basic GET/PUT endpoints per the spec. We'll optimize later.")
```

## Notes / Gotchas

- **Team lead only** — only team lead sessions can steer agents.
- **Agent must be active** — steering an idle agent fails with an error. Check `team_list` status first and confirm the agent's status is `streaming` before steering.
- **Injected as system message** — the steer message has high priority in the agent's context. The agent should see and respond to it promptly.
- **No guarantee of immediate effect** — the agent may be mid-tool-call when the steer arrives. It will pick up the message when it next reads from its context.
- **Be directive** — vague steering messages are less effective. Tell the agent exactly what to stop doing and what to do instead.

name: artifact_get
docs:
## Purpose

Retrieve the full content of a specific artifact by its ID. Returns the complete artifact including metadata (name, type, version, timestamps) and the full content (markdown, JSON, or other text).

This is how agents read design documents, review findings, test plans, and other artifacts to understand requirements and context.

## Parameters

| Name | Type | Required | Description |
|------|------|----------|-------------|
| `artifact_id` | string | **Yes** | The unique ID of the artifact to retrieve. Get this from `artifact_list`. |

## When to Use

- **Starting implementation work** — read the design doc to understand what to build.
- **Starting review** — read the design doc and any existing findings for context.
- **Checking requirements** — verify current requirements before making decisions.
- **Reading feedback** — check review findings or gap analyses for issues to address.

## When NOT to Use

- **Discovering what artifacts exist** — use `artifact_list` first to find artifact IDs.
- **Creating or updating artifacts** — use `artifact_create` or `artifact_update`.

## Examples

```
# Read the design document before implementing
artifact_list()  # → find the design-doc ID
artifact_get(artifact_id="6b4c5978-abcd-1234-ef56-789012345678")

# Check review findings for issues to address
artifact_get(artifact_id="review-findings-id-here")
```

## Notes / Gotchas

- **No truncation** — the full artifact content is returned regardless of size.
- **Use `artifact_list` first** — you need the artifact ID, which isn't predictable. Always list artifacts first to discover IDs.
- **Read before coding** — always read the design doc artifact before starting any implementation work. It contains architecture decisions, file changes, and acceptance criteria.
- **Version tracking** — the response includes the version number, so you can tell if the artifact has been updated since you last read it.

name: artifact_list
docs:
## Purpose

List all artifacts associated with the current goal. Returns metadata only (name, type, version, timestamps) — **not** the full content. Use this to discover what artifacts exist and get their IDs for subsequent `artifact_get` calls.

## Parameters

None.

## Returns

An array of artifact metadata objects:
- `id` — unique artifact identifier (use with `artifact_get`)
- `name` — human-readable name
- `type` — artifact type (design-doc, test-plan, review-findings, etc.)
- `version` — current version number
- `createdAt` / `updatedAt` — timestamps

## When to Use

- **Starting work on a goal** — discover what design docs, test plans, and other artifacts exist.
- **Checking artifact status** — see which required artifacts have been created.
- **Finding artifact IDs** — get the ID needed for `artifact_get` or `artifact_update`.
- **Verifying completeness** — confirm all required artifacts exist before completing the goal.

## When NOT to Use

- **Reading artifact content** — this returns metadata only. Use `artifact_get` for content.

## Examples

```
# Discover artifacts at the start of work
artifact_list()
# → [{ id: "abc123", name: "Design Document", type: "design-doc", version: 2, ... }]

# Then read the one you need
artifact_get(artifact_id="abc123")
```

## Notes / Gotchas

- **Metadata only** — content is not included. This keeps the response lightweight even when artifacts are large.
- **Call early** — agents should call `artifact_list` at the start of their work to understand what context is available.
- **Version numbers** — check version numbers to see if an artifact has been updated since you last read it.

name: artifact_update
docs:
## Purpose

Replace the content of an existing artifact with new content. The version number is automatically incremented, and the update timestamp is recorded. The previous content is overwritten — only the latest version is stored.

Use this to revise design documents, update review findings with new information, or refine test plans as the project evolves.

## Parameters

| Name | Type | Required | Description |
|------|------|----------|-------------|
| `artifact_id` | string | **Yes** | The ID of the artifact to update. Get this from `artifact_list`. |
| `content` | string | **Yes** | New content to replace the existing content entirely. Must include the complete updated document. |

## When to Use

- **Revising a design doc** — updating requirements, adding sections, or correcting architecture decisions.
- **Updating review findings** — adding new findings from a second review pass, or marking issues as resolved.
- **Refining a test plan** — adding test cases, updating coverage targets, or reflecting requirement changes.
- **Incorporating feedback** — updating any artifact based on team discussion or new information.

## When NOT to Use

- **Creating a new artifact** — use `artifact_create` for new artifacts.
- **Minor metadata changes** — there's no way to update just the name or type; only content is updatable.

## Examples

```
# Update the design doc with a new section
artifact_update(artifact_id="design-doc-id",
  content="# Tool Management API\n\n## Overview\n...\n\n## NEW: Caching Strategy\nAdd a 60-second TTL cache for GET /api/tools responses...\n")

# Update review findings after fixes were applied
artifact_update(artifact_id="review-id",
  content="# Code Review v2\n\n## Summary\nSecond pass after fixes.\n\n## Previous Issues\n- [RESOLVED] Missing input validation\n- [RESOLVED] No error handling on store write\n\n## New Issues\nNone.\n\n## Verdict\nApproved.")
```

## Notes / Gotchas

- **Full replacement** — the entire content is replaced. Include the complete updated document, not just the changes. Read the current content first with `artifact_get` if you need to preserve parts of it.
- **Version auto-increments** — each update bumps the version number by 1. Other agents can check the version to see if the artifact changed.
- **No version history** — previous versions are not retained. If you need to reference old content, copy it before updating.
- **All agents see updates** — the updated content is immediately visible to all agents on the goal via `artifact_get`.

name: team_abort
description: Force-abort a stuck agent that won't respond to steering
docs:
# team_abort

Force-abort a stuck team agent that won't respond to steering.

## When to use

Use `team_abort` when an agent is stuck in a long-running tool call (e.g. a bash command that hangs, or a tool that never returns) and `team_steer` has no effect because the agent can't process steering messages while blocked inside a tool.

## How it works

1. Attempts a graceful abort via RPC
2. Waits 3 seconds for the agent to stop
3. If still stuck, kills the agent process
4. Restarts a fresh agent on the same session (preserving conversation history)
5. Agent is now idle and ready for new prompts via `team_prompt`

## Parameters

| Parameter | Type | Required | Description |
|-----------|------|----------|-------------|
| session_id | string | yes | Session ID of the agent to abort |

## Example

```
team_abort(session_id: "abc-123-def")
? { ok: true, status: "idle" }
```

## Error cases

- **400** � Missing sessionId
- **403** � Session is not a member of this team
- **404** � Session not found
- **500** � Abort failed (internal error)
